text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH CONTRAST — PANCREATIC PROTOCOL (TRIPLE-PHASE)

CLINICAL INDICATION:
66-year-old female with biopsy-confirmed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the head and uncinate process, initially staged as borderline resectable based on SMA abutment and SMV narrowing on prior cross-sectional imaging. Patient has undergone biliary decompression via ERCP with FCSEMS placement and has now completed 4 cycles of modified neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin/irinotecan/leucovorin/5-FU). CA 19-9 has declined from a baseline of 1,204 U/mL to approximately 310 U/mL. Study performed for restaging and assessment of surgical candidacy.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed using a dedicated pancreatic protocol with triple-phase contrast enhancement (late arterial/pancreatic parenchymal phase, portal venous phase, and delayed venous phase). IV contrast: Omnipaque 350, 120 mL administered via power injector at 4 mL/sec. Oral contrast: negative (water) per protocol. Coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformations generated. Slice thickness: 1 mm collimation in the pancreatic bed, 3 mm for the remainder of the abdomen and pelvis. Images reviewed on workstation with dedicated vascular post-processing software. Comparison made to prior pancreatic protocol CT performed at the time of initial staging and prior MRI/MRCP.

FINDINGS:

PANCREAS:
The primary mass in the pancreatic head and uncinate process has undergone interval decrease in size and now measures approximately 3.0 cm in its largest transaxial dimension (previously 3.8 cm on the initial staging CT). The lesion retains its characteristic hypoenhancing appearance relative to the surrounding pancreatic parenchyma on the pancreatic parenchymal phase, with minimal internal enhancement on all phases, consistent with the known desmoplastic, hypovascular nature of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. The margins of the mass remain ill-defined with spiculated borders blending into the adjacent soft tissue and peripancreatic fat. There is an evolving zone of stromal fibrosis/treatment-related change at the periphery of the lesion that is difficult to distinguish from viable tumor on CT alone. The mass involves the pancreatic head from its superior aspect extending inferiorly through the uncinate process.

SUPERIOR MESENTERIC ARTERY (SMA):
The degree of tumor contact with the SMA has diminished compared with the prior study. The mass continues to abut the right lateral and posterior aspect of the SMA; however, the arc of contact is now estimated at approximately 120 degrees of the vessel circumference, representing a meaningful reduction from the approximately 180-degree abutment documented on the staging study. There is no definitive evidence of arterial encasement. The SMA lumen appears patent and of normal caliber without intraluminal thrombus or wall irregularity. Fat plane between the tumor and SMA, though still attenuated, shows slight improvement along the superior margin compared to prior.

SUPERIOR MESENTERIC VEIN (SMV) AND PORTAL CONFLUENCE:
The previously described focal narrowing of the SMV at the site of tumor contact has improved. The SMV now appears patent and without appreciable stenosis on the current study; the lumen maintains near-normal caliber throughout the length of its course posterior to the pancreatic head and through the portal confluence. No intraluminal thrombus is identified. The portal vein is patent. There is no collateral venous formation to suggest hemodynamically significant outflow obstruction.

CELIAC AXIS AND HEPATIC ARTERY:
The celiac axis, common hepatic artery, and proper hepatic artery remain uninvolved by tumor, without evidence of abutment or encasement. The splenic artery is patent along its course. No peri-arterial soft tissue thickening.

PANCREATIC DUCT:
The main pancreatic duct in the body and tail remains dilated, measuring approximately 6 mm in the body of the pancreas, slightly improved from the prior study (previously 7 mm at initial staging). The duct demonstrates smooth upstream dilatation consistent with obstruction at the level of the mass.

COMMON BILE DUCT AND BILIARY SYSTEM:
A fully covered self-expanding metallic stent is again demonstrated traversing the common bile duct and extending through the ampulla, with adequate luminal patency on current examination. The intrahepatic bile ducts are decompressed bilaterally without evidence of upstream biliary dilatation. No periductal enhancement or biliary wall thickening suggestive of cholangitis is seen. The gallbladder is not distended; no cholelithiasis.

LIVER:
The liver is normal in size and attenuation. No focal hepatic lesions are identified on any phase of contrast enhancement. No new hypodense or hypervascular nodules. The hepatic parenchyma enhances homogeneously without segmental abnormality. No biloma or fluid collection. The hepatic veins and inferior vena cava are patent.

SPLEEN:
Normal size and homogeneous enhancement. No splenic lesion or infarction.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in morphology. No adrenal lesion.

KIDNEYS:
Bilateral kidneys are symmetric in size and show normal cortical enhancement and excretion. No hydronephrosis. No suspicious renal mass. A stable 5 mm hypodense cortical lesion in the right kidney upper pole is unchanged and consistent with a simple cyst.

AORTA AND MAJOR VESSELS:
The aorta and iliac vessels are patent without aneurysmal dilatation. Mild atherosclerotic calcification of the aorta and its branches noted, as before.

LYMPH NODES:
The previously identified peripancreatic lymph nodes in the periportal and superior mesenteric regions remain present. The largest node in the peripancreatic distribution measures approximately 0.8 cm in short axis (previously 0.9 cm), now appearing slightly smaller. No discrete new adenopathy is identified in the retroperitoneum, mesentery, or pelvis. No lymph node meets size criteria for pathologic enlargement, though given the clinical context the peripancreatic nodes remain of indeterminate significance.

PERITONEUM AND OMENTUM:
No peritoneal nodularity, omental caking, or ascites. The peritoneal surfaces are smooth. No implants or thickened peritoneal reflections.

PELVIS:
No pelvic adenopathy or suspicious lesions. The uterus, bilateral adnexa, and bladder are unremarkable. No pelvic free fluid.

BONE WINDOWS:
Visualized osseous structures are without suspicious lytic or blastic lesions. Degenerative changes in the lumbar spine, as before.

IMPRESSION:

1. INTERVAL DECREASE IN SIZE OF THE PRIMARY PANCREATIC HEAD AND UNCINATE PROCESS MASS: The mass has diminished from 3.8 cm to approximately 3.0 cm in maximal transaxial dimension following 4 cycles of modified neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX, representing a meaningful reduction in tumor bulk. The lesion retains its characteristic hypoenhancing morphology.

2. IMPROVED RELATIONSHIP WITH THE SUPERIOR MESENTERIC ARTERY: The arc of contact between the tumor and the SMA has decreased to an estimated 120 degrees compared with approximately 180 degrees at initial staging. While the mass still abuts the SMA, there is no imaging evidence of frank encasement or luminal irregularity, and the degree of involvement has improved with treatment.

3. IMPROVED SMV PATENCY: The focal narrowing of the SMV previously noted at the site of tumor contact has resolved on the current examination, with the vessel now appearing patent and of near-normal caliber without hemodynamically significant stenosis.

4. NO DISTANT METASTATIC DISEASE: No new hepatic lesions, peritoneal implants, or pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified. The liver remains clear of metastatic deposits.

5. MILD IMPROVEMENT IN UPSTREAM PANCREATIC DUCTAL DILATION: The dilated main pancreatic duct in the body now measures approximately 6 mm, slightly reduced from the prior 7 mm, consistent with partial decompression effect.

6. PATENT BILIARY STENT WITH DECOMPRESSED INTRAHEPATIC BILIARY TREE.

7. STABLE SUB-CENTIMETER PERIPANCREATIC LYMPH NODE, SLIGHTLY REDUCED IN SIZE — indeterminate in the context of known malignancy.

Overall, the imaging findings reflect a favorable treatment response with reduction in tumor size, decrease in the degree of SMA contact, and restoration of SMV patency following neoadjuvant chemotherapy. The constellation of changes represents objective tumor regression compared to baseline. The patient's borderline resectable status based on vascular involvement has shown measurable improvement, though the SMA continues to be abutted and surgical resectability should be reassessed in the context of multidisciplinary evaluation and further treatment response.
